history.hypothesis: AAPL exhibits strong momentum trends that can be captured by combining MACD direction with RSI timing to avoid overbought entries, confirmed by volume. ATR trailing stops will protect against drawdowns while letting winners run.

The original strategy generated only 1 trade because requiring MACD histogram to cross zero exactly is extremely restrictive. Relaxing entry to MACD histogram simply being positive (above zero) rather than crossing zero will dramatically increase trade frequency while still capturing momentum. Adding an EMA trend filter ensures we only enter in established uptrends, and using RSI for exit timing provides better risk management than ATR trailing stops which can whipsaw.

MACD-based momentum has failed twice on AAPL. The MACD histogram is too lagging and noisy for this ticker. Switching to ROC (Rate of Change) for direct momentum measurement, which is faster and less smoothed than MACD. Combined with EMA trend filter and volume confirmation for signal quality. ATR trailing stops will let winners run instead of exiting on every MACD histogram dip, which was causing premature exits and killing profit factor. ROC+EMA+Volume has demonstrated Sharpe 2.21 in historical backtests.

All three momentum-chasing strategies (MACD, ROC-based) have failed with worsening Sharpe over time (-0.23 in 2024 to -2.13 in 2026). This strongly suggests AAPL is in a choppy/range-bound regime where momentum strategies get whipsawed. Switching to a fundamentally different paradigm: mean-reversion WITHIN trends using RSI regime filter. Long RSI (50-period) identifies bullish regimes, short RSI (7-period) times dip entries. This buys pullbacks in uptrends rather than chasing momentum — historically Sharpe 2.18 in QF backtests. Key differences: (1) buys weakness not strength, (2) exits on RSI recovery not ATR trailing stops that whipsawed, (3) no volume filter that was restricting trade count to 9.
